title: "Midnight Aster Problem v62.13"

  description: "At 22:45, the Midnight Aster ornament vanished from a sealed cabinet in the western gallery. Four suspects (1, 2, 3, 4) each had valid pass records near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in *all* their statements, while the other three speak only truths. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect presents five statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 cannot be guilty.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 4 was certainly absent at 22:40.'
      (3) 'I left the gallery at 22:35, so I wasn’t present at 22:45.'
      (4) '2 was still around the sealed cabinet at 22:40, contradicting 2’s denial.'
      (5) 'Either 4 never lingered beyond 22:30, or 3 is wrong about everything—but not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until 22:50, contradicting 1’s claim of 22:35.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4 must be completely honest.'
      (3) 'I was not near the Midnight Aster after 22:25, so I cannot be guilty.'
      (4) 'If 1 is truthful, then 3 must be lying in all statements.'
      (5) 'Either 4 is not the culprit, or 1’s entire timeline is wrong—but never both at once.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I never approached that cabinet after 22:20.'
      (2) 'If 4 is telling the truth, then 2’s third statement is false.'
      (3) 'Either 2 is lying, or 1’s 22:35 exit is incorrect—but not both.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs show me in the back corridor at 22:40.'
      (5) '1 was definitely at the cabinet close to 22:40, which conflicts with 1’s 22:35 departure.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was near the sealed cabinet at 22:30, contradicting 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is lying, then 2 must be fully truthful.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; a camera log places me in a different wing at 22:45.'
      (4) 'Either 2 hovered near the cabinet after 22:25, or 3’s entire account is false—but not both.'
      (5) 'If 3 is truthful, then 1’s timeline must be completely false.'

  question: \"Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Midnight Aster? Provide only one digit.\"

answer: >
  explanation: "Logical Derivation:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all five statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true; the others T(x).

  Testing each suspect as the universal liar:

  Case L(1):
    All of 1’s statements (1–5) are false => 1 did not leave at 22:35 => possibly present at 22:45 => suspicious. Checking 2,3,4 as truthful triggers conflict with statements like 3(5) or 4(1). Contradiction emerges.

  Case L(2):
    All of 2’s statements (1–5) are false => (3) false => 2 actually was near the Aster after 22:25 => suspicious. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful. Then 1(3) “Left 22:35 => not culprit”, 3(4) “I am not guilty => logs corridor,” 4(3) “I am not guilty => different wing.” So G(2)=true with no contradiction. 

  Case L(3):
    All of 3’s (1–5) false => 3 did approach after 22:20 => suspicious. Then 1,2,4 must be truthful; their statements conflict over times (e.g. 2(3), 4(1)). Contradictions arise.

  Case L(4):
    All of 4’s (1–5) false => that leaves 1,2,3 truthful, but leads to mismatch with 3(5) or 2(5). Contradictions result.

  Only L(2) => G(2) remains consistent: suspect 2 is the total liar and also the thief.

  final: '2'"
